name: Framing Effect — Disease Problem
description: The same decision framed as a gain ('200 saved') vs a loss ('400 will die') produces opposite risk preferences. Gain frames → risk-averse, loss frames → risk-seeking.

beliefs: I should make the best decision for everyone

stimulus.description: 600 people will die from a disease. Program A: 200 people will be saved (certain). Program B: 1/3 chance all 600 saved, 2/3 chance nobody saved. Framed in terms of lives SAVED.

expected.behavior: chooses the certain option (Program A) when framed as gains

sources: Tversky & Kahneman 1981 — The Framing of Decisions
